title: HTML Fundamentals
description: Learn the basic tags that build every web page: structure, headings, paragraphs, lists, links, and images.
congratsText: Great work! You now know the basics of building an HTML page.

sections.title: What Is HTML?

quiz.question: What does HTML mainly do on a web page?
options: It describes a page's structure and content
It calculates values and stores server data
It controls the page's loading speed
It encrypts data sent to the site

sections.lesson:
What Is HTML?
HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language. It is the standard language for building web pages. HTML tags describe the structure of a page: headings, paragraphs, lists, links, and more.
Key idea: HTML is not a programming language. It marks up content — it does not calculate or decide anything on its own.
Browsers read HTML and turn it into the page you see. Every website you visit sends HTML to your browser first.

sections.title: The Basic Page Structure

quiz.question: Where will the text "My Page" show up?
options: In the browser tab, not on the page
At the very top of the page
In the page's search results only
Nowhere — the browser will show an error

sections.lesson:
The Basic Page Structure
Every HTML page starts with a few required tags. The <!DOCTYPE html> line tells the browser which version of HTML to expect.
The main tags:
  • <html> wraps the whole page
  • <head> holds info about the page, not content you see
  • <body> holds everything visible on the page
<!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head> <title>My Page</title> </head> <body> Hello! </body> </html>
The <title> tag sets the text shown in the browser tab, not on the page itself.

sections.title: Headings

quiz.question: Why should you avoid skipping heading levels?
options: It can confuse screen readers and accessibility tools
The browser will refuse to show the page
It makes the page load much slower
It removes the page's title from the tab

sections.lesson:
Headings
HTML has six heading tags, <h1> through <h6>. <h1> is the biggest and most important. <h6> is the smallest.
Use headings for structure, not just size:
  • <h1> for the page's main title
  • <h2> for major sections
  • <h3> and smaller for sub-sections
<h1>Welcome</h1> <h2>About Us</h2> <h3>Our Team</h3>
Skipping levels, like going from <h1> straight to <h4>, can confuse screen readers and hurt accessibility.

sections.title: Paragraphs and Whitespace

quiz.question: How will the browser show the extra spaces in this code?
options: As a single space between the words
As all the spaces, exactly as typed
As a line break between the words
As an error message on the page

sections.lesson:
Paragraphs and Whitespace
Use <p> to group text into a paragraph. Browsers add space above and below each <p> automatically.
Whitespace collapsing: browsers ignore extra spaces and blank lines in your code. Many spaces become one space on the page.
<p>Hello World</p>
To force a line break inside a paragraph without starting a new one, use <br>.

sections.title: Text-Level Tags

quiz.question: What is the real difference between <strong> and just making text bold with styling?
options: <strong> carries meaning that screen readers can detect
<strong> only works inside a <head> tag
<strong> makes text bigger, not just bold
There is no difference at all

sections.lesson:
Text-Level Tags
Some tags change small pieces of text inside a paragraph. <strong> marks text as important. <em> marks text as emphasized.
Common text tags:
  • <strong> — bold, means "important"
  • <em> — italic, means "emphasis"
  • <mark> — highlighted like a highlighter pen
<p>This is <strong>important</strong> and this is <em>emphasized</em>.</p>
These tags carry meaning, not just looks. A screen reader announces <strong> text differently than plain text.

sections.title: Lists: Ordered and Unordered

quiz.question: You are listing steps to install software. Which list tag fits best?
options: <ol>, because the order of steps matters
<ul>, because bullets look cleaner
<li>, because it lists items directly
<ol> and <ul> work exactly the same

sections.lesson:
Lists: Ordered and Unordered
Use <ul> for a list where order does not matter. Use <ol> for a list where the order matters, like steps.
Both use <li> for each item:
<ul> <li>Milk</li> <li>Eggs</li> </ul> <ol> <li>Preheat oven</li> <li>Mix batter</li> </ol>
<ul> shows bullets. <ol> shows numbers that update automatically if you add or remove items.

sections.title: Nested Lists

quiz.question: Where must the inner <ul> go for the nesting to work?
options: Inside the outer <li>, before it closes
After the outer tag closes
Inside the <head> section only
It can go anywhere on the page

sections.lesson:
Nested Lists
You can put a list inside a list item to show sub-items. This is called nesting.
<ul> <li>Web Systems <ul> <li>HTML</li> <li>CSS</li> </ul> </li> </ul>
The inner list must sit inside the <li> of the outer list, before that <li>'s closing tag. Browsers usually show the inner list indented, often with a different bullet style.

sections.title: Links

quiz.question: What does target="_blank" do to this link?
options: Opens the linked page in a new tab
Makes the link text invisible on the page
Stops the link from working at all
Turns the link into a button instead

sections.lesson:
Links
The <a> tag creates a link. The href attribute sets where the link goes.
<a href="https://codeigniter.com">CodeIgniter</a>
Useful attributes:
  • target="_blank" opens the link in a new tab
  • An empty href="" links back to the same page
A visited link often changes color, so users can tell which links they already clicked.

sections.title: Images and Accessibility

quiz.question: Why should every tag include an alt attribute?
options: Screen readers use it and it shows if loading fails
It makes the image load faster on slow networks
It is required for the HTML to be valid at all
It changes the size of the image on the page

sections.lesson:
Images and Accessibility
The <img> tag shows a picture. It needs a src attribute pointing to the image file.
<img src="photo.jpg" alt="A student presenting a project">
Always include alt text:
  • Screen readers read alt text out loud for blind users
  • Alt text shows if the image fails to load
A missing or wrong src makes the image fail to load — alt text is the only thing users still get.

sections.title: Putting It All Together

quiz.question: What is the fastest way to check the exact HTML code behind any page?
options: Right-click the page and choose View Page Source
Open the browser's download folder
Search the page's title on the web
Zoom into the page as far as possible

sections.lesson:
Putting It All Together
A real page combines all these pieces: the basic structure, headings, paragraphs, lists, links, and images, all inside one <body>.
<!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head><title>My Site</title></head> <body> <h1>Welcome</h1> <p>This is my <strong>first</strong> page.</p> <ul><li>About</li><li>Contact</li></ul> <a href="about.html">Learn more</a> </body> </html>
You can check your own raw code anytime with View Page Source in your browser — a fast way to see exactly what you typed.
